episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Rethinking Privacy Through Indigenous Data Sovereignty and Rethinking Privacy Through Indigenous Data Sovereignty, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Whose sovereignty does Canada's 'sovereign AI' plan actually serve, and does it advance or undermine Indigenous data sovereignty? Topics include: Distinction between national sovereignty and Indigenous data sovereignty in AI strategy, OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, Possession) and implementation gaps, Data governance for relational Indigenous knowledge versus Western individual-privacy frameworks, AI for climate and accountability structures versus technical optimization, Guardian programs and knowledge extraction without adequate resource allocation, Parallel versus integrated Indigenous AI infrastructure models.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Friendship Society

articles.title: InFocus: What’s behind Canada’s ‘sovereign’  AI plan?
articles.summary: InFocus: What’s behind Canada’s ‘sovereign’  AI plan?

discussion.central_question: Whose sovereignty does Canada's 'sovereign AI' plan actually serve, and does it advance or undermine Indigenous data sovereignty?
discussion.riley_position: Canada's sovereign AI strategy prioritizes national economic competitiveness over Indigenous data sovereignty, and the historical pattern of building infrastructure first and consulting later means Indigenous governance will likely be retrofitted rather than foundational.
riley_key_evidence: Canada's AI strategy iterations have been organized around national economic competitiveness, not community sovereignty
OCAP principles have existed since the late 1990s but remain consistently underimplemented across infrastructure projects
Pattern of build-first-consult-later seen in broadband, environmental monitoring, and health data systems
discussion.casey_position: Indigenous communities should seriously consider building parallel, independently-governed AI infrastructure rather than defaulting to integration into national systems, with genuine funding and authority—not just consultation roles.
casey_key_evidence: The Tŝilhqot'in built their own land monitoring infrastructure after the William case rather than waiting for provincial systems
Community-developed tools like Jill Ruep's beading app prove the proof-of-concept when governance is baked into development from the start
MMIWG data undercounting demonstrates consequences of communities not controlling information systems that document violence against them
discussion.resolution: They reached substantial agreement that community ownership at the architecture stage (not consultation stage) is the proven model, and that the technical barriers to Indigenous-controlled AI infrastructure are now lower than ever—though genuine funding remains the critical gap.
topics_covered: Distinction between national sovereignty and Indigenous data sovereignty in AI strategy
OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, Possession) and implementation gaps
Data governance for relational Indigenous knowledge versus Western individual-privacy frameworks
AI for climate and accountability structures versus technical optimization
Guardian programs and knowledge extraction without adequate resource allocation
Parallel versus integrated Indigenous AI infrastructure models
